summary: DeVault argues that FOSS developers who want to make money must build the business before or alongside the software, not after. Because open source requires surrendering monopoly rights, building software first and hoping to monetize later risks losing first-mover advantage to someone else who does the business work. He emphasizes that making money in FOSS is possible but rarely happens by accident—it requires deliberate planning around company structure, customers, pricing, and revenue model. The post outlines several monetization approaches including support contracts, consulting, education, SaaS, and donations, noting that donations are the hardest path.
key_points: FOSS requires surrendering sole monetization rights, so delaying business-building risks letting others capture the value of your work
Making money in FOSS requires deliberate planning—company structure, target customers, pricing, and revenue model must be thought through from the start
Different software projects suit different revenue models: support contracts, consulting, books/courses, SaaS, or donations
If you choose not to build a business around your FOSS project, others are fully entitled to do so without you
money_quotes: It's a common mistake to do this the wrong way around: build the software, then the business.
FOSS requires you to surrender your sole monetization rights.
If you lapse in your business-building efforts and your software project starts making someone else money, then they're not at fault for "taking your work" — you gave it to them.
I've often said that you can make money in FOSS, but not usually by accident.
Don't just build your project and wait for the big bucks to start rolling in.
You need to incorporate your business, hire an accountant, and do a lot of boring stuff which has nothing to do with the software you want to write.
And if you skip this step, someone else is entitled to do all of this boring work, then stick your software on top of it and make a killing without you.
key_insight: In FOSS, the software is freely available to everyone, so the competitive advantage lies entirely in building the business around it—skip that work and someone else will do it without you.
